responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Value Judgments in Psychoanalytic Theory and Practice

Joseph Lichtenberg M.D. and Sidney Lytton M.D.

Sidney Lytton

Joseph D. Lichtenberg, M.D.
Sidney M. Lytton, M.D.
11 East 90th Street
New York, New York 10028

Sidney Lytton  

LICHTENBERG INTRODUCED THE PANEL BY DEFINING a value judgment as "the assertion of a belief in the guiding virtue of a principle or a belief in the existence of a concrete entity for which substantiating evidence is either lacking, incomplete, or opposed by facts." To give an example of this definition, he used the statement: "Jehovah is the one supreme god," which is impossible to substantiate because evidence is lacking. He then referred to Freud's concept of specific forms of psychic energy and noted that, analogously it is impossible to validate or invalidate this theory. He showed how such a concept could progress, because of adherence to authority, from being a working hypothesis to being a value judgment which, by virtue of repetition, becomes dogma.

After discussing a number of areas of both practice and theory where he feels value judgments enter, Lichtenberg presented his belief that the negative influence of value judgments derives from two factors: the seductive assumption that none exists; and the disguising effect of clinical success, resulting from different therapeutic approaches.

The assumption that value judgments either do not exist or do not require in-depth exploration may derive from the belief that by virtue of training and discipline, psychoanalysts are free from making value judgments.

Held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association. New York, December, 1981.

Panelists: John E. Gedo, William W. Meissner, Robert Michels.

147

The borderline syndrome is comprised of different subsyndromes, subtypes, or subcategories. Thus, Otto Kernberg has described in detail a number of borderline disorders of varying degrees of character pathology (even though not in the chapter he contributes to this book, except tangentially), and Roy Grinker did subdivide his borderline syndrome into four types, extending from the psychotic to the neurotic border of functioning. In recognizing such subcategories, both authors conceived them as a continuum of pathology, emphasizing, all the same, the independence of the condition as such.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Unfinished Journey

By Louis Breger. London: Routledge & Kegan Paul, 1981, 220 pp., $15.00.

Alan Skolnikoff M.D.

Alan Skolnikoff

Alan Z. Skolnikoff, M.D.
San Francisco, California

Alan Skolnikoff  

This book describes how Freud was influenced in the development of his theories by the cultural and scientific milieu of his time. The author's main thesis is that although Freud was partially able to transcend these scientific and cultural values, many of his theories are conservative and rooted in the biases of his period. The "unfinished journey" could be pursued if we seek out, examine, and remove biases that have limited the development of a universal psychoanalytic theory.

We begin with an exploration of Freud's background as a scientist. The author feels that this has influenced him too much in the direction of a physicalist-materialist approach, with reason and objectivity being overvalued. The result is a metapsychology that is too mechanistic, limited by the ideals of nineteenth-century physics.

In Chapter 3, "Psychoanalysis Is Not a Science," the author would follow George Klein and Roy Schafer in an attempt to remove the superstructure of metapsychology and to tie psychoanalytic principles directly to clinical theory. He contrasts "older" scientific models with "modern" ones. Praise is given to some of the new models, but the emergence of a new false scientism is associated with others. Special praise is given to John Bowlby (p. 42) who the author feels is able to dispel the mechanistic conception of the oral drive and the pleasure principle and modify them with data from ethology, information and systems theories, and direct observations of mothers and infants. Bowlby thereby develops the more humanistic and adaptive concept of attachment behavior. G. Bateson is also praised for using information and systems theory to illuminate the bizarre family communication patterns in schizophrenics, changing the conception of mental illness from an internal process to an interactive one. Any theory that attempts to objectify and dehumanize the individual too much is not to be trusted. Hence the author prefers to consider that psychoanalysis is not a science, though it shares some qualities with the scientific approach.

[[RunningHead]]

Editor's Introduction

Theodore Shapiro M.D.

Theodore Shapiro

Theodore Shapiro  

AS THE NEW EdITOR OF THE Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, I greet the members of the Association and our readers. The Journal has been passed on to me, and the Board in excellent shape: We boast the broadest circulation of any existing psychoanalytic journal both in number of readers and in breadth of its distribution. I thank the retiring Editor, Dr. Harold P. Blum, as I am sure you do, for providing us with his maximum effort over these many years to make the Journal what it is. The excellent standards of scholarship and care in selection of original manuscripts are difficult achievements, but he did the job with concern and foresight. In his own words, he had excellent help from many, including the members of his various Boards. They are also to be congratulated. I shall carry on with the last Board selected by Dr. Blum, and hope to win their confidence and cooperation in continuing the job that was started by my predecessor. I am grateful also to Dr. John Frosch, a mentor since residency years, who began this Journal and continues to lend support to me. To both former Editors, my thanks for your efforts and for your confidence.

Now, what lies ahead? I am sure that experience in the position will alter my untutored initial plans—I think that is a sure rule of life. However, I do have some ideas about what a journal is, and what I shall try to accomplish: A journal is first and foremost a medium for exchange among colleagues and coprofessionals. New ideas, themes, and scientific demonstrations should make up the bulk of what we record in the Journal. It also should be a vehicle for discourse, and even for controversy as the times, empirical findings, and models of thought change. Psychoanalysis is growing as every science does. Our intellectual stamina becomes resurgent in the wake of new observations and advances within and outside of psychoanalysis, in other sciences. Thus, I would encourage potential contributors to submit well considered hypotheses based on good observations, new and original case presentations documented by careful forthright discussion, statistical demonstrations of psychoanalytic propositions, well documented scholarly reinterpretations of existent models, and methodologically sophisticated applied analytic papers.

[[RunningHead]]

Internal World and External Reality: Object Relations Theory Applied

By Otto Kernberg. New York: Aronson, 1980, 376 pp., $27.50.

Calvin Settlage M.D.

Calvin Settlage

Calvin F. Settlage, M.D.
Sausalito, California

Calvin Settlage  

With object relations theory and the concept of internalized object relations as focus and theme, Kernberg's central objective in this volume is to delineate the psychoanalytic conceptualization of dyadicstage psychic development, and to demonstrate its bearing on later developmental stages, on couple and group relationships, and on psychopathology and the theory of treatment. Contemporary object relations theory, as distilled and defined by Kernberg, is an integral part of contemporary ego psychology, and his application of it has the second objective of integrating rather than selectively revising or discarding the clinical and metapsychological concepts of traditional Freudian theory (pp. ix and 17-18).

In the first of the volume's four parts, Kernberg approaches these objectives through a review and critical examination of object relations theory. In addition to summarizing the clinical and research findings that led him to his present theoretical stance, he discusses the historically early concepts of Melanie Klein and Fairbairn, and the more recent concepts of Jacobson and Mahler. He notes that his developmental view is in basic accord with the views of Jacobson and Mahler, and points mainly to positive correlations, and to some differences, between Mahler's and his developmental schema.

In part two, an updated review of normal and pathological narcissism in middle age precedes exposition of object relations theory as it bears on psychoanalytic technique and the theory of psychoanalytic psychotherapy. Kernberg states his preference for a cohesive, holistic psychoanalytic technique and indicates that his theoretical addition applies both to presystemic (dyadic stage) and systemic (oedipal stage) pathology. His theory of psychoanalytic psychotherapy is presented with quite precise clinical indications, objectives, and techniques, and is compared with the psychoanalytic method and technique.

[[RunningHead]]

The Value of Extratransference Interpretation

Leo Stone M.D. and Eugene Halpert M.D.

Eugene Halpert

Leo Stone, M.D.
Eugene Halpert, M.D.
2 Parkside Drive
Great Neck, New York 11021

Eugene Halpert  

LEO STONE SET THE HISTORICAL, THEORETICAL and clinical framework of the panel's topic. He noted that whereas the centrality of transference interpretation has been repeatedly emphasized by analysts, other interpretations have been assigned an exclusively adjunctive or preparatory function and have rarely been discussed. He emphasized the centrality and ultimate essentiality of transference interpretation and of the transference neurosis as the phenomenon that epitomizes the analytic experience. It is the transference neurosis, he commented, that permits the profundities of object relations and fundamental conflicts to become clearer and more accessible to understanding and interpretation than they would be in any other relationship. Despite this conviction, stone noted that clinical questions persist. Are interpretations elicited by other issues superfluous? Or diversionary? Or feeble? Or possibly just ancillary? Is material from other sources indispensably informative, providing a matrix for the transference emergence and understanding, and in that sense alone, useful? Or can such interpretations contribute in themselves to significant "structural change"? Or do they sometimes include effective transference references via the principle of multiple appeal?

Held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association. New York, December, 1981.

Panelists: Harold P. Blum, Paul Gray, Nathan Leites.

137

[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Psycholinguistics

By Theodore Shapiro. New York: Plenum, 1979, xiv + 179 pp., $22.50.

David Freedman M.D.

David Freedman

David A. Freedman, M.D.
Houston, Texas

David Freedman  

Ours is a talking profession. Language—more specifically oral language—is the principal instrument with which we work as therapists, our principal research tool, and the medium we use for communication. On the face of it, linguistics should be numbered among those basic sciences on which psychoanalysis depends. Yet it is the case that during some 80 years of psychoanalytic development the study of

180

language qua language and the application of insights derived from the study of linguistics have played a minor role. This is the case despite the fact that, as Shapiro points out, "as therapists we depend on metaphorical representation" (p. 107). Indeed, it is a truism that without the figures of speech, whether in the form of dream symbols or as elements in free associations, psychoanalysis would be impossible.

Clinical Psycholinguistics seems to provide some understanding of why this paradox exists. The author cites de Saussure who invoked the distinction between, "… la langue (language) and its particular form, la parole (speaking) to inform us that speech is a particular mixture of physical, physiological, psychological and social factors which may concern us in our investigation of language as an institution and as a formal system. Speaking is studied by different methods than is language. Language may be viewed as a systematic, codified area for study which was by and large ignored until Saussure effected his distinction" (p. 15). Speech, Shapiro notes, is only one of several instrumentalities (the list also includes patterned behavior, gesture, affect, and sign language) that can be used as a medium for communication. Linguists, however, apparently limit their efforts to understand communication to the study of the structure of oral languages and the patterns of their change over time. It is by virtue of this emphasis that their interests and those of psychoanalysts, who are users rather than students of oral language, diverge. The divergence is perhaps best illustrated by Chomsky's assumptions concerning linguistic competence. According to Shapiro this author regards linguistic competence as an innate given inherent in the average expectable equipment of normal human beings. For him, language in effect unfolds no matter what the circumstances under which the individual develops. Shapiro observes that linguists who make this assumption, "… in their purest working conditions prefer to have a sentence on a 3 x 5 card or a blackboard without its context—without its conditions of performance" (p. 17). Also, one might add, without concern for the idiosyncratic conditions that lead to the unique properties of any given individual's modes of communication.

[[RunningHead]]

Anti-Judaism: A Psychohistory

By Ernest A. Rappaport. Chicago: Perspective Press, 1975, 312 pp., $12.50.

Mortimer Ostow M.D.

Mortimer Ostow

Mortimer Ostow, M.D.
Riverdale, New York

Mortimer Ostow  

While the psychoanalytic method and theory were applied to the study of Jewish religion and scriptures in the third and fourth decades of this century, analysts did not direct their attention to anti-semitism until after World War II—with one exception. Sigmund Freud addressed it in Moses and Monotheism. Upon careful study, a historian friend of mine concluded that the major esoteric purpose of that essay was to defend the Jews against their detractors.

After the Continental analysts escaped to the United States and England, a number of them tried to master their trauma by composing essays or books on the nature and etiology of anti-semitism. This book, though published only in 1975, posthumously, belongs to the collection of psychoanalytic responses to the Holocaust.

Ernest Rappaport, who died shortly before the book was published, an alumnus of the University of Vienna Medical School and Buchenwald Concentration Camp, studied and practiced psychoanalysis in Chicago for many years. He has contributed heavily and creatively to the psychoanalytic literature.

Dr. Rappaport seems to have taken a good deal more trouble to inform himself on the history of the Jewish people than his colleagues who had published on anti-semitism previously, just as Freud gives evidence of having prepared for Moses and Monotheism by voluminous study of Jewish history and literature. The book consists of a synoptic survey of the history of anti-semitism, interrupted by fairly detailed descriptions of some of the most egregious episodes of anti-semitism, and biographies of some of the most egregious anti-semites. There are individual chapters on Paul, Augustine, Luther, Hitler, Eichmann and Stalin; on the Crusades and the Spanish Inquisition; on the legend of the Wandering Jew and the many ritual blood libels.

701

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Explorations of Technique: Discourse on the Theory of Therapy

Edited by Harold P. Blum. New York: International Universities Press, 1980, viii + 468 pp., $25.00.

Joseph Sandler Ph.D.

Joseph Sandler

Joseph Sandler, Ph.D.
Jerusalem, Israel

Joseph Sandler  

This important book first appeared as a supplementary issue of the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, and has now reappeared in book form with three previously published papers (by K. R. Eissler, Phyllis Greenacre, and René A. Spitz) added. Many of the papers bear witness to the current conflict between the striving to

182

remain "classical" and the pressure to adapt our psychoanalytic procedures to take account of the very real gains in clinical understanding of the past decade or two. This conflict, which the historian may one day judge to reflect healthy development, understandably finds expression as opposition between necessary "innovations" on the one hand, and "dangers to be guarded against" on the other.

The book starts with a group of papers on insight. The first is by Anna Freud, who draws attention to the special problems encountered by the child analyst because the analytic method "runs counter to the child's own natural inclination." It is only as he matures that the need for technical modifications is diminished. There follows a paper by Hansi Kennedy proposing a developmental view of insight. In child analysis our interpretations help the child resolve conflicts holding up his development, rather than give insight into the past. While the child may not yet possess an internalized analyzing function, understanding of the present external and internal situation can be considered to be insight. Next, a scholarly review of the role of insight in psychoanalysis by Peter Neubauer leads him to place special emphasis on cognitive factors and on the role of integration and synthesis in insight formation. A thoughtful and stimulating paper by Harold P. Blum on curative and creative aspects of insight, reflecting the close link between theory and technique, completes the quartet. Insight does not necessarily lead to change, but it fosters structural change, which has to be continually assessed by the analyst. Insight, Blum notes, is the condition for and consequence of a progressive analytic process.

[[RunningHead]]

Existentialism and Psychoanalysis

By Charles Hanly. New York: International Universities Press, 1979, 298 pp., $25.00.

Gilbert Rose M.D.

Gilbert Rose

Gilbert J. Rose, M.D.
Rowayton, Connecticut

Gilbert Rose  

Sartre's special type of existentialism, phenomenological philosophy, is the main focus of this book by Charles Hanly, a University of Toronto philosopher equipped with psychoanalytic training. His method is to translate phenomenological principles into the concrete psychological experiences to which they refer. The author then shows that while these are accurate descriptions of certain psychological states, they are by no means fundaments of human existence on which

212

to build a philosophy. On the contrary, these states are readily recognizable as surface manifestations of unconscious conflict (the existence of which existentialism does not recognize—but more on this later). For example: Existentialism's emphasis on the realization of oneself only in action bespeaks a primitive level of object relations; existentialism's description of the relations between the sexes is rife with sadomasochism and body-image anxiety, leaving no room for relatively conflict-free mutuality; the boredom, despair, loneliness, and emptiness of existentialist case histories tell of depression; the existentialist idea that one's consciousness never coincides with itself sounds like estrangement; the emphasis on "nothingness" reminds one of the "as-if" personality and other profound identity disorders.

Sartre's position starts with atheism. There being no God, there is no fixed human nature that determines one to act. Man is totally free and entirely responsible for what he makes of himself. Human reality is conscious choice. Neurosis is the problem of making free choices in one's existence. Experiences such as anguish, vertigo, nausea, and shame are based in consciousness itself—consciousness facing up to an intrinsic property of human existence: the equipossibility of choosing life or death.

According to Hanley, existentialism thus assigns to consciousness a nuclear role in mental life. The unconscious simply does not exist. Consciousness is the person's existence. It is an absolute starting point; it exercises unimpaired surveillance and includes what we usually attribute to a combination of observation, thought, and cognition. The ego is a secondary phenomenon formed by and perpetually renewed by a reflection on the spontaneity of consciousness.

Aaron Esman

New York, New York

Aaron Esman  

One of the more interesting phenomena of recent years has been the burgeoning interest in psychoanalysis among literary critics. As psychoanalysts find themselves increasingly drawn to a view of their discipline as a hermeneutic one, more literary scholars appear to be turning to it as a means of enhancing their understanding of texts and readers' responses to them.

In this densely written, carefully conceived and generally impressive book, Dr. Skura delineates the various ways in which psychoanalytic thinking has been and can be used in the service of literary criticism. In doing so, she elaborates, extends, and comments on the work of her many predecessors and peers in the field—Trilling, Burke, Holland, Habermas, Derrida among them—as she formulates her own proposal for a new approach to the enterprise.

SUMMARY

There is a spectrum of normality and psychopathology in which we see the presence of denial in fantasy, action, and word. The underlying ego distortions, such as the overuse of a denying fantasy in reality testing, will determine the clinical appearance of this mechanism. Traumatic experiences in the first 18 months are a determining factor in the development of these ego distortions. Clinical material is presented that supports a theory of elation put forward by Lewin (1950) who suggests that denying elations are repetitions of childhood dreams with their adult elaborations. In Mrs. A., the denying elations occurred particularly when experiences of castration anxiety or object loss became intolerable.

Daniel Schwartz  

This comprehensive book on schizophrenia repays analytic study. The work uses Dr. Pao's vast experience as a psychoanalyst with the unusually difficult schizophrenic patients treated at Chestnut Lodge. As befits the Director of Psychotherapy at this institution, Pao's endeavor in this book is appropriately broad.

Schizophrenic Disorders reorganizes what T. S. Kuhn, the historian of science, would call the "explanatory conceptual paradigm" of schizophrenia. It places the integrative functions of the ego and the cohesion of the self at the center of that paradigm's assembled data. The schizophrenic's ego, Pao says, finds its integrative functions overwhelmed in the course of conflict. Organsimic panic, an unmanageable terror, ensues which inactivates workable defenses and adaptive ego functions. Primitive ego functions emerge which supplant the inactivated ego.

Narcissism: Psychoanalytic Essays

By Béla Grunberger. New York: International Universities Press, 1979, xix + 311 pp., $22.50.

Arnold Richards M.D.

Arnold Richards

Arnold Richards, M.D.
New York, New York

Arnold Richards  

In Narcissism: Psychoanalytic Essays, Béla Grunberger presents a series of papers written between 1956 and 1971, together with a preface and introduction written in 1971 for the French edition. Most of the papers appeared originally in the Revue française de Psychoanalyse and are available in English translation for the first time. Encompassing a variety of theoretical and clinical concerns, the essays are bound by a common theme set forth in the introduction: "the source of all the various forms of narcissism," Grunberger writes, is a prenatal state of elation, an intrauterine state of omnipotence and self-sufficiency from which man was "traumatically expelled and that he never ceases longing to recapture" (pp. 10, 12). This hypothesized state, basically continuous with Freud's "primary narcissism," is presented as the central explanatory construct by which we can understand (a) object relations during the various developmental stages, (b) psychopathology, (c) the analytic process, and (d) the mechanism of analytic cure. This grand hypothesis, however, is nowhere subjected to confirmation or disconfirmation in the clinical situation. Presumably, Grunberger's conviction rests on his belief in the heuristic value of the hypothesis; clearly the notion of prenatal elation has helped him make sense of his clinical experience and the human condition generally. If we adopt the view that psychoanalytic theories are "created equal," and that the pragmatic test of a theory is its ability to promote the analysis, then the "truth value" of the hypothesis is not really at issue. It is simply Grunberger's myth of early experience, on a footing with the myths of Freud, Melanie Klein, and others before him.

But "truth value" considerations aside, Grunberger's construct fails even the test of internal coherence. In general, the idea takes off from the physiological notion that the fetus exists in a tension-free equilibrium.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalysis of Organizations

By Robert de Board. London: Tavistock, 1978, 158 pp., $16.50 (paperback, $6.95).

Harry Levinson Ph.D.

Harry Levinson

Harry Levinson, Ph.D.
Cambridge, Massachusetts

Harry Levinson  

This informative book is mistitled. It does not tell one how to psychoanalyze organizations (if, indeed, one could), nor does it report on organizations that have been analyzed. Rather, the author traces the origins of many group-process activities in organizations to their psychoanalytic heritage. His intention is to illustrate the influence psychoanalytic theory has had on understanding the way people behave in groups and organizations. The author, who has spent more than 20 years working in a variety of organizations, is on the faculty of the Henley Staff College in England and apparently has had significant relationships with the Organizational Studies Group of the Tavistock Institute.

He begins with the works of sociologist Max Weber, social psychologist Kurt Lewin, Freud, industrial engineer F. W. Taylor, and executive Henri Fayol through four major social experiments in organizations from 1920 to 1939. This introductory chapter establishes the frame of reference which is the background for his psychoanalytic focus.

His base is Freud's Group Psychology and the Analysis of the Ego, followed by a discussion of S. Ferenczi's contributions on introjection and transference. These set the stage for understanding group and organizational behavior in terms of a Kleinian orientation to intrapsychic processes.

He describes Melanie Klein's emphasis on the twin psychological mechanisms of projective and introjective identification, and splitting and denial. He traces her influence on W. R. Bion, a fundamental theorist of group processes; on Trist, Bamforth, Rice, and Miller, of the Tavistock Institute's Organizational Studies Group, whose research comprises the basis for what is now called socio-technical systems; on the work of Jaques, Menzies, and Miller and Gwynne who see organizations as a defense against psychotic anxiety. Inbetween these chapters he traces the life and work of Kurt Lewin, the fundamental theorist of contemporary social psychology whose early work was an effort to conduct controlled empirical research on psychoanalytic theory. De Board describes evolution of T-groups which came out of Lewin's work, and the Tavistock leadership conferences, shaped significantly by Rice, which derived from them and integrated those group activities with Kleinian thinking. He outlines general systems theory which the socio-technical theorists integrated with their Kleinian orientation.

SUMMARY

The complexities of insurance coverage and peer review as they intrude into the analytic situation were discussed. The basic question was raised of whether becoming acceptable to major health providers through the peer-review program is truly a compromise of the analytic pact and a threat to the future of psychoanalysis. Practical as well as technical considerations facing the analyst when he is confronted with having to write a report to the patient's insurance company were elucidated. Countertransference feelings were emphasized. A detailed case illustration was presented to demonstrate that peer review, as

794

it relates to third-party payment, need not compromise an analysis.

[[RunningHead]]

The Clinical Use of the Manifest Dream

Sydney Pulver M.D. and Owen Renik M.D.

Owen Renik

Sydney E. Pulver, M.D.
Owen Renik, M.D.
2300 Sutter Street (Suite 201)
San Francisco, California 94115

Owen Renik  

SIDNEY PULVER INTRODUCED THE PANEL BY POINTING TO conflicting attitudes about the use of the manifest dream in clinical work which have existed side by side in psychoanalysis since Freud's time. On the one hand, Freud called the manifest dream the "royal road to the unconscious" and spoke of certain dream symbols directly revealing unconscious meanings. On the other hand, Freud cautioned that in most cases we cannot determine the significance of the dream from its manifest content alone, but must obtain the dreamer's associations.

Following initial interest in the manifest dream on the part of Freud and his early colleagues, there set in a depreciatory attitude toward the manifest dream, punctuated by occasional studies like those of Sharpe and Erikson.

Connected to this controversy about the clinical use of the manifest dream is a controversy about the definition of terms: What is the manifest dream, as distinct from the dream report, the dream experience, and secondary revision? Sleep laboratory researchers tend to have a different view of proper usage than psychoanalytic clinicians.

A. Scott Dowling's presentation addressed itself to the form of the manifest dream. Form can be divided into dream morphology, the regular structure of individual dream elements, and dream syntax, the regular arrangement of dream elements vis-à-vis one another. In contrast to dream form is the semantics of the dream, which consists of the meanings attached to the content of dream elements, to be deciphered by free association.

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association. Boston, May, 1982.

Panelists: A. Scott Dowling, John E. Mack, Stanley Palombo.

Patrick Mahony  

SUMMARY

As we broaden our frames of reference, Freud's genius looms larger. An adequate appreciation of Freud accordingly necessitates an examination of how he read and wrote and how Strachey translated him. Despite the enormous amount of psychoanalytic commentary, the subject of his writing qua writing still remains untapped to a considerable extent. By deepening our awareness of the nuances and implications of the manner in which Freud wrote, we correspondingly increase our knowledge of his original mind and the way he understood psychoanalysis.

The Dream in Clinical Practice

Edited by Joseph M. Natterson, New York: Aronson, 1980, 498 pp., $30.00.

Frederick Lane M.D.

Frederick Lane

Frederick M. Lane, M.D.
New York City

Frederick Lane  

Natterson's professed intention in gathering these essays on dreams is to furnish a "readily accessible contemporary work on dreams" and to generate interest in the use of dreams in various treatment modalities applied to psychopathological states. The work is addressed to psychoanalysts and to psychotherapists of various disciplines in the mental health field, as well as to students in training.

In the first section "Clinical Theory of Dreams," an essay is presented which seems representative of the interpretive approach of several articles in this book. In "The Manifest Dream and its Latent Meaning," by Louis Breger, the author presents a Kantian view of the dreaming experience as knowable to the dreamer only through construction by various ego functions such as self- and object schemata, cognitive skills, affect states. The manifest dream is the product of the construction of experience (in Piagetian terms), much as the child constructs his world from the mélange of sensations that form his experience. There is no latent content in the dream. Content is seen only in the manifest dream. What is latent is a series of meanings: "the analyst … provides a construction … and a more meaningful explanation for the dream in the surrounding context of the patient's associations, relationships, and life history." Breger's case illustration shows these meanings as constructed not only from the patient's associations, but also from the transference context, the therapist's total knowledge of the patient's life, his own allegorical interpretations, images, and associations. He decries the attempt of many analysts to find the latent dream thought and force its meaning upon the patient. In my view, substituting a more sweeping selection of data and using a mode of intuitive interpretation or "construction" for the more disciplined procedure of using the dreamer's associations as the main fulcrum for interpretation, will more likely eventuate in misleading dream interpretations. This presents the danger, as with other inexact interpretations, of being used by the patient in the service of resistance.

Humberto Nagera  

Volume 6 of Adolescent Psychiatry is indeed an extraordinary volume within a remarkable series. The variety and richness of the papers it contains, many of them by noted colleagues, constitutes a special and rare treat. Many have been written by noted colleagues.

The twenty-nine papers are organized in four sections. The first one deals with "General Considerations Regarding Adolescence." John Bowlby's contribution which appears here, titled "Attachment Therapy and its Therapeutic Implications," reviews in some detail the development of the attachment theory, contrasting it with the dependency theory and emphasizing the features of specificity, duration, ontogeny, learning, organization, etc. Bowlby underlines his view that attachment theory can be tested systematically. It incorporates much psychoanalytic thinking, and adopts a number of principles derived from ethology and control theory. He dispenses with the concepts of psychic energy and drives. He finds this model of attachment theory more compatible with neurophysiology and developmental biology and he believes that it conforms well to the criteria of scientific discipline.

In this same section, is Klumpner's "Review of Freud's Writings on Adolescence," which was his Presidential address to the Chicago Society for Adolescent Psychiatry in 1973. Another is P. S. Weesberg's paper in this section "Demographic, Attitudinal and Practice of Adolescent Psychiatrists in the United States," which contains many useful tables that, as the editors of the volume point out, demonstrate that adolescent psychiatry is "well and healthy, thank you very much!"

The second section contains seven papers on "Developmental Issues and the Adolescent Process," including a paper by S. V.

Fritz Morgenthaler, who practices in Zurich, presented the bulk of this book in 1977 to the René Spitz Society in Munich. 677

Dr. Palazzoli and associates, working as a team since 1972 in the Milan Center for Family Studies, developed and formulated a plan for the treatment of families who have a child or young adult suffering from a "serious mental disorder," and applied it on an experimental basis to fifteen families. Five of the families had children aged five to seven who were exhibiting serious psychotic behavior, and ten families had children varying in age from ten to twenty-two diagnosed as acute schizophrenics of recent origin. The authors emphasize that this book is only a preliminary report and that publication is "undoubtedly premature." Palazzoli emphasizes that the term schizophrenia signifies to her not an illness of an individual in accordance with the traditional medical model, but a particular communication pattern inseparable from the communication patterns observed in the family "in schizophrenic transaction." She believes that the old traditional causal-mechanistic view of phenomena (i.e., behavior and symptoms) employed in medicine must be abandoned and a systemic orientation adopted, representing a new epistemological model largely based on the conception of feedback. This would allow the therapist to see the members of a family as elements in a circuit of interaction in which every member influences the others, but is in turn influenced by them. Therefore it is an epistemological error to say that the behavior of one individual is the cause of the behavior of other individuals within the family system.

The working method comprises a team of four psychiatrists, two women and two men. One heterosexual couple is in the conference room with the family while the other pair of colleagues is in the observation room. From case to case, the couples may alternate or vary roles or partners from observer to direct interaction with the family. Generally, a series of ten sessions for an hour per session at monthly intervals is considered an optimal period for therapy. Although some time variations were made, in no instance was a total of twenty sessions exceeded.

For over 20 years, Offer and his associates have been engaged in the systematic study of the psychological development of normal adolescents. Their findings, along with those of a number of other investigators such as M. Rutter, G. E. Vaillant and J. Adelson, have set in train a substantial reconsideration of traditional views, in particular those derived primarily from clinical observation.

The present volume adds a further dimension to the work reported in such books as The Psychological World of the Teenager and From Teenage to Young Manhood. It presents the analysis of responses of adolescents of both sexes and from a number of environmental settings to a self-assessment instrument (The Offer Self-Image Questionnaire), devised to tap the subjects' views of their own personalities and "coping" capacities. Data were obtained not only from the modal suburban Chicago public school youth of previous studies, but also from parochial and Eastern private-school students, incarcerated delinquents, psychiatric patients, and adolescents hospitalized for physical illness. In addition, "normal" young people in Ireland, New Zealand, and Israel were included among the subjects of this study.

Melvin Scharfman  

This volume, like its predecessors in this series, is an anthology covering many diverse aspects of adolescent psychiatry. It was published in conjunction with the International Year of the Child, and, in the editors' introduction, is described as attempting to emphasize the subtle interrelations between current world political ties and the developmental and clinical studies with which the practicing psychiatrist is concerned. The editors state their impression that more and more clinical presentations are concerned with severe psychopathology as this volume suggests. They express their hope that insights derived from such psychopathology as well as increasing knowledge about adolescent processes will broaden our knowledge of psychopathological social structures and help to explain the reaction of masses to charismatic and, in some instances, dangerous and destructive leaders, although this volume does not specifically elaborate it.

The volume itself is divided into five sections, contains 38 separate contributions. Of these, two are special sections—one dealing with the effects of social and cultural change, and another dealing with legal and psychiatric perspectives on delinquency and acting out. These sections, and one on psychopharmacology, contain several interesting papers, but are, in general, more geared to psychiatrists than psychoanalysts. The broad scope of this volume, encompassing contributions from different theoretical perspectives and a wide-range of treatment approaches, is part of its appeal to a variety of mental health workers dealing with adolescents. At the same time, it makes a comprehensive in-depth review of the many contributions a task beyond the scope of this review. I shall address myself largely to those contributions dealing with general considerations of adolescence — developmental issues, psychopathology, and therapeutic approaches.

There are several papers of interest to psychoanalysts in the section entitled "Adolescence: General Considerations." An article by Peter Blos, "Modifications in the Classical Psychoanalytic Model of Adolescents," continues his contributions to the understanding of the adolescent process. Blos takes issue with the idea that central in the adolescent process is the revival and renewed resolution or transformation of the Oedipus complex, in accordance with what he terms classical recapitulation theory.

The Oceanic Feeling: The Origins of Religious Sentiment in Ancient India

By J. Moussaieff Masson. Dordrecht, Holland: D. Reidel, 1980, 213 pp.

Irving Harrison M.D.

Irving Harrison

Irving B. Harrison, M.D.
White Plains, New York

Irving Harrison  

George Devereux, in a preface, calls this slender volume "a landmark in the history of applied psychoanalysis," in which "the world of ancient India comes to life." For the psychoanalytic clinician, however, and for the serious student of psychoanalytic theory, Masson's essays transcend the application to Indian culture and make a substantial contribution to an aspect of psychoanalytic theory and practice which is presently in ferment.

The religious world of ancient India seems to have incorporated a malaise shaped by, and having shaped, specific varieties of narcissistic pathology. Masson delineates the regressive defenses that attend such narcissistic disorders, particularly the denial of depressive anguish, and he explicates the religious, mystical and (anti-) therapeutic effects of furthering such regression. He places "cosmic narcissism" (as advocated by Kohut) among the latter. The religious—and mystical—affect is characterized by the oceanic feeling.

The concept of an oceanic feeling had been advanced by the mystically inclined Romain Rolland for Freud's consideration; Rolland extolled it as profoundly beneficent. Masson documents convincingly the morass of severe depressive psychopathology among the Indian religious leaders who originated and extended the concept. Elements of their religious thought emerge like symptoms, with superego-syntonic elements amalgamated to repressed sadistic and masochistic urges, but drenched in the narcissism characteristic for such depression. Masson states (p. 76) "the longing for the ocean as an overt symbol of eternal peace is always a covert reference to suicide."

Masson demonstrates, incidentally, the antianalytic character of a number of currently advocated modifications in psychoanalytic technique which aim at giving the analysand evidence of support but tend to further regression into narcissism. His study highlights the crucial role of early trauma and the subsequent struggle to cope with its after-effects.

In this scholarly book, Donzelot, a psychoanalytically oriented sociologist, traces the historical development in France of attitudes, customs, practices, and laws involving the family. He makes a central point that psychoanalytic concepts have prevailed because they offer regulatory but noncoercive approaches instead of the heavy-handed solutions proposed by other groups like the Marxists and feminists.

In the first chapter, "The Preservation of Children," the author notes that beginning in the eighteenth century, physicians and other authorities questioned the educative customs of the century, particularly the utility of foundling hospitals, where 90 per cent of the children died; the rearing of children by nurses; and the artificial education of rich children.

Reading the psychoanalytic literature on the topic of aggression should be a corrective educational experience for anyone who thinks that psychoanalysts adhere to a monolithic theory. Consonant with this state of affairs, Parens declares his intention to question some basic assumptions of current psychoanalytic theory pertaining to aggression. One of his basic, unquestioned assumptions in doing so is that the examination of aggressive manifestations in the first few years of life will reveal the "simplest" forms of aggression and elucidate its subsequent development.

Parens proceeds in the first of three sections to examine the psychoanalytic theory of aggression and to offer his own reformulation of it.

Tearfulness: A Psychoanalytic Interpretation

Edwin Wood M.D. and Constance Wood Ph.D.

Constance Wood

Edwin C. Wood, M.D.
Constance D. Wood, Ph.D.
5300 San Jacinto Street (Suite 150)
Houston, Texas 77004

Constance Wood  

SUMMARY

In our view, the tearful feeling state is occasioned by the ego temporarily threatened with being inundated by complex memories and affects. For the moment, the ego cannot provide appropriate satisfying and integrated verbal expression. Words and verbal thinking are delayed. In this brief period, a controlled regression occurs during which the ego relates the current "precipitating experience" to temporally older conflictual experiences in the presence of the current object as a transference object and with a series of relationships so closely connected to that experience that they are interchanged in whole or part by the less selective, regressed, ego operations.

The conscious experience may be sadness, gratitude, love, pride, or joy. But beneath these, there is the controlled regression, which interrupts verbal expression while the ego works through the earlier related conflicts, now remembered.

The tearful feeling, a step in the direction of overt weeping, primitively expresses the frustration of an early wish for relief

134

from pain, the pain of the imbalance of a complex conflict. The tearful feeling both expresses the wish for relief of pain and, at the same time, interrupts current verbal expression until the needed defense operations reduce the threat of ego disruption by working through old conflicts once again.

Our thesis, then, is that within the analytic or psychotherapeutic hour the patient's feeling of tearfulness is a "too much" ego phenomenon that leads to a psychophysiological outlet.

Psychoanalysis and Language

Stanley Olinick M.D.

Stanley Olinick

Stanley L. Olinick, M.D.
2819 Ellicott Street, N.W.
Washington, D.C. 20008

Stanley Olinick  

It has often been commented that writing and printing have resulted in the loss of something very important, a direct linking and knowing between persons. Such linking is counterfeited in the pseudo intimacy of the telephone and of television; a pseudo intimacy may also be attained in the reading of some books.

To be sure, it has not been writing and printing alone that have led to this loss. It is the full impact of the industrializing of society, including the increased size and mobility of the population, which has also contributed heavily to this distancing of communication.

Does this direct knowing and linking return in the psychoanalytic situation? Is there a partial replacement or a renaissance of a more direct and primitive communication that had been lost? If so, what are the consequences?

Preliterate peoples have had different perceptions and orderings of experience than have members of Western industrial society. This is expressed and crystallized by their languages; in turn, language forms and patterns the disparate experiences of the groups. Technically advanced and sophisticated as modern languages are, they contain fossilized residues of earlier modes of thinking, feeling, and expression.

Freud and his Patients

Edited by Mark Kanzer and Jules Glenn. New York: Aronson, 1980, 452 pp., $25.00.

Peter Blos M.D.

Peter Blos

Peter Blos, Jr., M.D.
Ann Arbor, Michigan

Peter Blos  

The book under review is the second in a series of four volumes celebrating the Downstate Psychoanalytic Institute's 25th Anniversary. It is a collection of articles by members of the faculty examining various facets of the five classical cases: Freud's patients of the title. Well known to all analysts, these cases continue to be a vital part of every candidate's psychoanalytic education—and rightly so. They display, in addition to the clinical content, the noted hallmarks of Freud's style. To this day they are impressive for their clarity of expression, sharpness of focus, and taut narrative line. One welcomes the opportunity to reexamine these cases and observe again the nuances of Freud's thought: what he did and did not know, the paradoxical keenness of his mind with regard to some matters and his obtuseness in others, what he considered important and what he overlooked. Above all one notes how often his clinical observations and inferences presaged later theoretical formulations.

In the preface the editors state their desire "to set forth some of the significance of [Freud's] explorations in a form which students and teachers of the broadest aspects of human relationship" can utilize. They do not claim "systematic or comprehensive review" but rather "pragmatic" exploration. And they promise, in addition to the articles themselves, a concluding summary attempting "a synthesis of the historical, technical and conceptual aspects of all the case histories." An ambitious project indeed.

The volume opens with an introduction by Jules Glenn in which the five cases are appropriately placed in the dual perspectives of psychoanalytic history and the development of psychoanalytic theory.

Beyond Insight: Deep Rhymes, Deeper Reasons

By Sumner L. Shapiro. New York: International Universities Press, 1979, $20.00.

Lawrence Deutsch M.D.

Lawrence Deutsch

Lawrence Deutsch, M.D.
Englewood, New Jersey

Lawrence Deutsch  

At a time when many consider psychoanalytic thinking outmoded and when a myriad new therapies have sprung up, Dr. Shapiro's book, which illustrates Freudian tenets and their applications, is both welcome and refreshing.

This is the author's second volume and a sequel to Moment of Insight, published in 1978. In the first volume, clinical excerpts were presented which demonstrated that insight can lead to psychological change. One of the criticisms of the first book was that it made the psychoanalytic process look too easy. Consequently, Dr. Shapiro undertook a more ambitious project in the new volume, seeking to demonstrate the dynamic and genetic aspects of conflict.

Some of the illustrations presented are very similar in nature to those in the first volume in that the vignettes show the development of insight by the analyst which is then shared with the patient. For example, "Hang Up" describes a young man who had been raised on a farm. The child suffered from a sense of discomfort and indignity at having to use the family outhouse. The patient, now a young adult, was unable to use a public telephone. Dr. Shapiro recognized that the telephone booth, with which the patient was unfamiliar, reminded him of the dreaded outhouse.

Another case report, entitled "Alas Poor Yorick" presents in narrative form illustrations of the Oedipus complex, the development of defenses, and the utilization of psychoanalytic counseling. Dana, who lost her mother when she was five, developed a very close attachment to her father. She married a solid and unexciting man, and they moved to an apartment close to where her father resided. A short time later, Dana's father was killed in a freak accident. Dana did not show signs of mourning; in fact she seemed to adjust well to the loss. Some months later she became depressed and withdrawn, and Dr. Shapiro elected to work with her husband, who had come to discuss his wife's plight. He helped the husband to develop an understanding of his wife's difficulties, her need to express rage, and her sensitivity to a symbolic representation of her father's grave.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis in France

Edited by Serge Lebovici and Daniel Widlöcher. New York: International Universities Press, 1980, xiv + 451 pp., $35.00.

Burness Moore M.D.

Burness Moore

Burness E. Moore, M.D.
Atlanta, Georgia

Burness Moore  

Without a common, abstract, symbolic language by which international scientific exchange would be facilitated, attempts to present and integrate divergent schools of thought in psychoanalysis are seriously hampered. This problem, evident from the beginning of psychoanalysis, has received increasing attention in the past few years. There now exists an International Publications Committee, composed of editors of psychoanalytic journals throughout the world, which has initiated an exchange of abstracts of papers from the various journals. Psychoanalysis in France, available in an excellent English translation, is one product of another type of informal exchange—of anthologies of the psychoanalytic literature of a particular country and culture. A similar anthology of papers from the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, edited by Harold Blum and translated into French, has already appeared and has been well received in France.

The volume under review here includes only purely psychoanalytic contributions, excluding applications of psychoanalysis to psychosomatic medicine, art, philosophy, history, anthropology, and politics, which are extensive in France. Moreover, French psychoanalysts, the editors say, have a marked preference for theoretical subjects, especially metapsychology and applied psychoanalysis, while clinical psychoanalysis and technique are relatively neglected. In their selection of 21 papers, the editors have corrected this imbalance by including three that deal with development, seven devoted to metapsychology and theory in general, six discussing problems of technique, and five clinical contributions. In this review, specific mention will be made of only a few of the contributions. An anthology presenting so many authors, subjects, and viewpoints within a single field is more conducive to an impressionistic than a detailed examination.1

Lebovici and Widlöcher offer their critique in a brief, perspicacious introduction, all the more valuable because their extensive knowledge of psychoanalysis throughout the world enables them to make valid comparisons. They observe that from 1960 on what is sometimes referred to as a "return to Freud" has been marked by great interest in the nuances of translation and semantic discussions.

In this work, the author discusses in general theoretical fashion with a superabundance of quotations from the literature but, regrettably, with only a few very perfunctory clinical vignettes, some aspects of the depressive syndrome with particular emphasis on the genetic ones.

According to Haynal, its beginnings are to be found in the progressive separation of the infant from the mother which occurs in the process of individuation. "The feeling of having lost the object or, rather, certain aspects of the object, together with the resignation to this loss, to the extent that the child is unable to recreate the object within the self, marks the beginning of the depressive affect. This can be overcome if the mourning process develops harmoniously but it may give way to a vulnerability to depression if events become traumatic."

This feeling of having lost the object marks the transition from marcissism to object relations and involves the relinquishment of infantile omnipotence through the experience of impotence, helplessness, and hopelessness which occurs when the loss assumes a character of finality, the patient finding himself confronted with a fait accompli

[[RunningHead]]

A Psychoanalytic Study of Migration: Its Normal and Pathological Aspects

León Grinberg M.D. and Rebeca Grinberg M.D.

Rebeca Grinberg

León Grinberg, M.D.
Rebeca Grinberg, M.D.
Francisco Gervas 11
Madrid 20, Spain

Rebeca Grinberg  

SUMMARY

Without maintaining that it always follows the same steps, we could say that the migratory process passes through several phases.

1. The feelings that prevail are those of intense sorrow for all that has been abandoned or lost, fear of the unknown, and the very profound experiences of loneliness, privation, and helplessness. Paranoid, confusional, and depressive anxieties occupy the scene in turn.

2. This stage may be followed or replaced by a manic state in which the immigrant minimizes the transcendental significance of the change in his life or, on the contrary, magnifies the advantages of the change and overvalues everything in the new situation, disdaining what has been lost.

3. After a variable period of time, nostalgia appears, and sorrow for the lost world. The immigrant begins to recognize feelings previously dissociated or denied and becomes capable of "suffering" his pain ("growing pains") while, at the same time, he becomes more accessible to the slow and progressive incorporation of elements of the new culture. The interaction between his internal and external world becomes more fluid.

4. Recovery of the pleasure of thinking and desiring and of the capacity for making plans for the future, in which the past is regarded as such and not as a "lost paradise" where one constantly longs to return. In this period, it could be considered that mourning for the country of origin has been worked through to the maximum extent possible, facilitating integration of the previous culture into the new culture, without the need to renounce the old. All of this promotes an enrichment of the ego and the consolidation of a more evolved sense of identity.

WOLF INTRODUCED THE PANEL by stating that his initial interest in analysts' neutrality was nourished by growing recognition that failed neutrality or inappropriate neutrality were the most important contributors to his analytic failures, and he acknowledged the help of discussions with Heinz Kohut in formulating his own ideas on analytic ambience.

Wolf remarked on his astonishment in finding that the term "neutrality" is neither in the Glossary of Psychoanalytic Terms and Concepts, nor in the index of the Standard Edition or the Gesammelte Werke. Psychic Trauma and Masochism

Jules Glenn M.D.

Jules Glenn

Jules Glenn, M.D.
8 Preston Road
Great Neck, New York 11023

Jules Glenn  

SUMMARY

Three analyses are presented in which acute trauma (over-whelming internal or external stimulation so great as to preclude the patient's utilizing his usual defenses adequately) in childhood contributed to the development of masochism. The patients later attempted mastery through repetition, reversal, and erotization, and employed regression as a defense against feared oedipal wishes.

[[RunningHead]]

The Multiple Meanings of Masochism in Psychoanalytic Discourse

Franklin Maleson M.D.

Franklin Maleson

Franklin G. Maleson, M.D.
The Jefferson Building
1015 Chestnut Street
Philadelphia, Pennsylvania 19107

Franklin Maleson  

SUMMARY

The term masochism was originally used in a narrow, specifically sexual sense (referring to perversions), but has come to

353

encompass for many analysts an exceedingly broad and variably determined range of clinical phenomena that bear no consistent relation to sexual excitement. In addition, these expanding clinical perspectives have long been entangled with Freud's shifting metapsychological constructions designed to ground masochism in instinctual theory. As a result, the term is used with little consistency and at varied levels of abstraction: loosely descriptive, dynamic, theoretical. It is not always explicit which meaning is intended or what inferences, if any, are meant to be drawn from the term. When used in its broadest sense, masochism may falsely suggest dynamic similarity between diverse phenomena, and is often ambiguous with respect to the presence or absence of underlying erotic or perverse excitement.

In the clinical situation, the categorization of material as masochistic may evoke an associated set of genetic, dynamic, or theoretical ideas which may alter the perception, ordering, and interpretation of clinical data. Particularly conducive to this subtle steering process are the enduring influence of the early concept of feminine masochism and the related concept of sadistic and masochistic paired opposite component sexual instincts—a theoretical, physicalistic, linear concept incompatible with the clinical model of overdetermination and multiple function.

This paper includes a review of the terminological and conceptual history of masochism, briefly touches on the parallel history of sadism, and offers some provisional definitional solutions.

[[RunningHead]]

Narcissism and Superego Development

Phyllis Tyson Ph.D. and Robert Tyson M.D.

Robert Tyson

Phyllis Tyson, Ph.D.
Robert L. Tyson, M.D.
737 Pearl Street (Suite 208)
La Jolla, California 92037

Robert Tyson  

SUMMARY

To elucidate the role of the superego in the maintenance of narcissistic equilibrium, we reviewed Freud's ideas about narcissism and the superego as well as the relevant theories of Kohut, Kernberg, and certain ego psychologists. These latter authors offer an alternative mode of understanding narcissism more consistent with Freud's structural theory, one in which

94

signal affects and superego functioning play a central role in normal development and in the pathogenesis of narcissistic disturbances. Early steps in superego formation were then examined schematically to elucidate the interaction of environmental influences and emerging psychic structure. We suggested that the first step in a developmental line toward superego formation is based on the affective qualities experienced in the course of self-object differentiation. Subsequent steps examined were introject and ego-ideal formation; compliance with the object; compliance with the introject; identification with the introject and the ego ideal; and finally, with oedipal resolution, the integration of superego nuclei into a progressively structuralized autonomous superego system. This system achieves growing independence from the drives and from pressures from early introjects during the course of latency, and functions to maintain the demands of the conscience and the standards of the ego ideal; rewards or punishments result when these demands and standards are or are not met. The final stage briefly considered here was the revision, modification, and elaboration of moral codes and the ego ideal as part of the adolescent process. Narcissistic vulnerabilities at various stages were pointed out in an attempt to stress that a particular clinical picture in later phases of development or adulthood may derive from any of several developmental points of origin and from one or more etiological factors.

[[RunningHead]]

Deconstructing the Manifest Dream

Stanley Palombo M.D.

Stanley Palombo

Stanley R. Palombo, M.D.
35 Wisconsin Circle
Chevy Chase, Maryland 20815

Stanley Palombo  

SUMMARY

A major part of the analyst's task is to discover the basis for the patient's misidentification of his present life situation with significant but threatening events of his earlier life, now repressed and inaccessible to conscious recall. Reconstructing the patient's history is a crucial step in this process of discovery, but the dynamic relation between the present and the past must be reconstructed as well.

The structure of the manifest dream contains the key to this relation. The imagery of the dream is a composite of experiential materials drawn from important drive-related events of the present and the past. The complex formed by the manifest dream and the patient's associations provides the analyst with data about both of these distinct sets of drive-related experiences.

As Freud's discussion of his Mélusine dream illustrates, one associative thread can be traced to an experience that incorporates a conflicted current wish. Another thread will lead to

419

an experience in which a repressed wish of childhood has been expressed. Where the two associative threads converge, in the composite imagery of the dream, the basis for the identification between the wishes of the present and the past will be exposed.

An understanding of the structure of the manifest dream helps to clarify some of the important theoretical issues left unresolved in Freud's writings. These include: the function of the day residue and the mechanism through which it is formed, the relation of the screen memory to the associative process, and the differing roles of condensation and displacement in dream construction and free association. A simple procedure is described for enhancing the recovery of the significant childhood memories whose details have been incorporated into the composite imagery of the manifest dream.

[[RunningHead]]

An Example of Self-Analysis

Helen Beiser M.D.

Helen Beiser

Helen R. Beiser, M.D.
180 North Michigan Avenue
Chicago, Illinois 60601

Helen Beiser  

SUMMARY

The literature on self-analysis indicates that it is a desirable process to cultivate postanalytically for continued self-growth, analyzing countertransference problems, and dealing with symptoms or problems of living that might arise after formal analysis is completed. A number of techniques are described, the choice of which is probably dependent on the personality of the individual who wishes to pursue self-analysis.

I describe an episode of self-analysis occurring near the end of the analysis of a woman who aroused in me an unusual degree of positive feeling, at least part of which can be considered

11

countertransference. After she had aroused negative feelings in me, she was able to deal in an affectful way with strongly repressed impulses, actually acted out in early childhood, to steal from her mother. This stimulated dreams in me that allowed me to work out guilt for hostility to my mother. A dream representing stealing her purse altered long-standing compulsive thoughts I had known meant guilt to my mother. The self-analysis was not undertaken to deal with a countertransference problem, but arose out of it to deal with a symptom, or old conflict, 25 years after the completion of formal analysis.

[[RunningHead]]

Fear of Humiliation

Arnold Rothstein M.D.

Arnold Rothstein

Arnold Rothstein, M.D.
275 Central Park West
New York, New York 10024

Arnold Rothstein  

SUMMARY

This paper explores the ontogeny of fear of humiliation, conceived of as an important organizing affect-laden fantasy in certain narcissistic personality disorders. The influence of the pleasure of the parental object in sadistically humiliating is emphasized in the overdetermined genesis of this fear. While, in elaboration of Loewenstein's (1957) and Eidelberg's (1959) contributions, "seduction of the humiliator" is a fundamental defensive process observed in masochistic characters, identification with the humiliator is a sadonarcissistic defense observed in work with certain narcissistic personality disorders. The countertransference potential to enjoy humiliating such analysands, as well as the defensive functions of fear of humiliation, are noted.

[[RunningHead]]

The Relation Between Psychoanalytic Theory and Psychoanalytic Technique

Robert Wallerstein M.D. and Arnold Richards M.D.

Arnold Richards

Robert Wallerstein, M.D.
Arnold Richards, M.D.
40 East 89th Street
New York, New York 10028

Arnold Richards  

ROBERT WALLERSTEIN INTRODUCED THE PANEL by commenting on the threefold unity of psychoanalysis. It is at once a general theory of the mind, a method of investigating its processes and understanding its nature, and the treatment of its disorders. Analysts take as almost an article of faith that theory is therefore closely linked to technique and that change in theory will be directly translated into change or modification in technique. Analysts maintain this conviction even though as clinicians they often observe change without insight and insight without change. However, counting against the view that theory and technique "lock securely together," but rarely reflected on as such, sits the knowledge that though there have been significant changes in theory over nearly a century of psychoanalysis, yet we are hard-put to detail the ways in which technique has altered in their light. Wallerstein's "complaint" is that in practice there has been less correspondence between theory and technique than is usually claimed. For this reason a fresh and open-minded look at the relation between theory and technique, "whether necessary and consequential, or contingent and conditional, or loose and optional, or quite haphazard and unnecessary," is indeed appropriate and timely.

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Boston, May, 1982.

Panelists: Leo Rangell, Otto Kernberg, Paul Ornstein; Discussants: Robert Wallerstein, Arnold Richards.

[[RunningHead]]

Son and Father

Peter Blos Ph.D.

Peter Blos

Peter Blos, Ph.D.
56 West 91st Street
New York, New York 10024

Peter Blos  

SUMMARY

I have traced in my deliberations the mutual influence of drive and ego development throughout the male child's dyadic and triadic father relatedness as it proceeds within a changing soma and social surround during the first two decades of life. I have made the effort to conceptualize the normal developmental progression in male personality formation with explicit reference to the fate of the boy's dyadic father relationship as well as his negative Oedipus complex in general. These considerations, restricted as they are in scope and gender, assign to the dyadic father complex a nuclear role in neurosogenesis as well as recognize in it an etiological factor in relation to specific forms of psychopathology throughout the male life cycle.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis at One Hundred: Beginnings of Maturity

Arnold Cooper M.D.

Arnold Cooper

Arnold M. Cooper, M.D.
525 East 68th Street, P-174
New York, New York 10021

Arnold Cooper  

SUMMARY

We are celebrating a most significant birthday. Whether we reckon our age at one hundred or ninety, it is a time of professional maturation, an occasion for enormous pride in the achievement of psychoanalysis. That achievement was made under special circumstances, which have given a unique shape to our enterprise. But as we achieve maturity, we have begun to change in new and not entirely predictable ways, which call for new perceptions, the development of new attitudes, and the creation of new structures for our functioning. A new form of educational institution deserves our most urgent attention.

It is predictable that the pace of change will quicken. It is part of our psychoanalytic heritage that we show the courage to face new realities and to find new adaptations which will insure the recruitment of bright young minds, the continuing advance of psychoanalysis, and our growing contribution to human welfare. I am optimistic for our future.

[[RunningHead]]

Observations on Assessment of Analyzability by Experienced Analysts

Joan Erle M.D. and Daniel Goldberg M.D.

Daniel Goldberg

Joan B. Erle, M.D.
11 East 68th Street
New York, New York 10021
Daniel A. Goldberg, M.D.
1148 Fifth Avenue
New York, New York 10128

Daniel Goldberg  

SUMMARY

Some specific approaches to the study of analyzability have been identified in this study as productive and feasible. First, it was possible to enlist a group of experienced analysts as a source of clinical material—the data and their own evaluations. Second, the assessment of analyzability can only be made at the end of the treatment. Third, the classification of analysands as either "analyzable" or "unanalyzable" warrants further differentiation. Cases of patients who develop an analytic process may have variable outcomes: some conclude successfully with no modification in technique; some conclude with significant limitations in the analytic resolution; others require an appropriate modification in technique at some point. Cases of unanalyzable patients also have a variable outcome: some develop no useful treatment situation; in others the patient's unsuitability for analysis is manifest very early and a change to the appropriate psychotherapy is made.

The study of how analysts make their evaluations and the subsequent course of those treatments seems useful in providing information on our work as psychoanalysts pertinent to practice, teaching, and research. Our experience suggests that a prospective study would be advantageous: data reported at the time of assessment could be more complete and less subject to retrospective distortion; definitions and criteria could be established in advance; important questions could be more clearly defined and explored as the material develops (by co-investigators as well as the analyst). This might include significant differences between analysts, the identification and impact of

736

modifications, and the study of cases started in psychotherapy and subsequently seen in analysis.

[[RunningHead]]

The Two Concepts of Action and Responsibility in Psychoanalysis

Wynn Schwartz Ph.D.

Wynn Schwartz

Wynn Schwartz, Ph.D.
Department of Psychology Wellesley College
Wellesley, Massachusetts 02181

Wynn Schwartz  

SUMMARY

I have presented two major concepts of action and responsibility which have a role in psychoanalysis and have been confused in the recent literature. Intentional action is the general action concept concerning consciously, preconsciously, or unconsciously enacted goal-directed behavior. Deliberate action is a type of intentional action, performed consciously or perhaps preconsciously, that involves a choice among specific possible ways of achieving a goal. Persons are ethically responsible for their deliberations and also responsible as the agent, deliberate or not, of the manifestations of their personal characteristics. Responsibility for mere agency is not the same as ethical responsibility, but since it is easy to confuse these two concepts

571

they may be hazardous as features of clinical dialogue. Finally, "free will" and "determinism" are discussed as issues that historically attend the above subject matter. "Free will versus determinism" is argued to be a false issue properly replaced by Freud's actual interest in the "overdetermined" multiple motivations of behavior.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Perspectives on Emptiness

Steven Levy M.D.

Steven Levy

Steven T. Levy, M.D.
Emory University Psychoanalytic Clinic
1711 Uppergate Drive, N.E.
Atlanta, Georgia 30322

Steven Levy  

SUMMARY

A clinical example illustrates the thesis that the experience of feeling empty, like any other mental event, can be understood in terms of the conflicting wishes, prohibitions, compromises, and gratifications that color any experience in ways that clarify its meaning. Theoretical hypotheses which explain mental

402

events and experiences as the result of deficiencies of structures are difficult to translate into therapeutic practices. This is especially true in the case of the experience of emptiness which, in and of itself and often vigorously, asserts an absence of content. The equating of deficiencies of structures, however formulated, with deficiencies in mental content or activity can result in unconsciously joining the empty patient in repudiating important aspects of internal life, maintaining ultimately pathological gratifications, and often contributing to treatment stalemates in which the "absence of content" is often preferred to the presence of frightening wishes, fantasies, and memories.

[[RunningHead]]

The Relation Between Masochism and Depression

Arnold Rothstein M.D. and Joseph Caston M.D.

Joseph Caston

Arnold Rothstein, M.D.
Joseph Caston, M.D.
2710 Broderick Street
San Francisco, California 94123

Joseph Caston  

IN HIS INTRODUCTION, ROTHSTEIN NOTED that the present panel, in inquiring into the relation between masochism and depression, follows the suggestion made in an earlier panel by Arnold Cooper: that it is now appropriate to devote a period of exploration to masochism, as has been done for narcissism.

Rothstein observed, in his literature review, differences in emphasis between oedipal versus preoedipal, and biologic versus psychodynamic considerations. Freud made no specific comment relating the two. His contributions regarding depression are an extension of the construct of narcissistic drive. Freud stressed that depression resulted from narcissistic injury due to object loss, compensated for by an identification, on which the "critical activity of the ego" then discharged its "sadism and hate." Confusion derives from the attempt to understand the relation between depression, which most analysts consider to result from oral or preoedipal deprivation, and the superego, which most think of as "heir to the Oedipus complex."

Although Freud's earliest work on masochism was within the pleasure principle, he definitively explained masochism by resorting to the death instinct, a hypothesis strongly influenced by biological considerations. He distinguished "an erotogenic, a feminine, and a moral masochism," the erotogenic form being fundamental to the others, and the psychical derivative of the death instinct. Yet in descriptions of clinical aspects of masochistic fantasies his emphasis was clearly oedipal.

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Boston, May, 1982.

603

[[RunningHead]]

A Note on Loss, Pain, and Masochism in Children

Jules Glenn M.D.

Jules Glenn

Jules Glenn, M.D.
8 Preston Road
Great Neck, New York 11023

Jules Glenn  

THE HUMAN PSYCHE IS SO COMPLEX that it is impossible to tease out individual factors and demonstrate that they alone produce certain psychic configurations. Overdeterminism is the rule. In this paper, I shall concentrate on the role of early separation from parents and its influence on depression and masochism. Inevitably, I shall supplement the too-simple picture that emerges with an outline of other determinants. I shall indicate briefly the role of oedipal guilt (Freud, 1924); (Brenner, 1959), the observation of the primal scene (Freud, 1905), the need to gratify sadistic parents (Berliner, 1947); (Bernstein, 1957) and the mechanisms of control of dangerous objects and drives (Panel, 1956); (Loewenstein, 1957); (Eidelberg, 1968); (Panel, 1981).

In referring to depression, I am including the affect of sadness (with its ideational and feeling aspects and physiological concomitants) and the diagnoses, often labeled depression, in which that affect appears. The diagnoses include reactive depression, endogenous depression, mourning, grief, and melancholia.

This is a modified version of a paper presented at the Panel, "An Exploration of the Relationship Between Masochism and Depression" at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Boston, May, 1982.
