[[RunningHead]]

Oedipus in the Stone Age

Theodore Lidz M.D. and Ruth Lidz M.D.

Ruth Lidz

Theodore Lidz, M.D.
Ruth W. Lidz, M.D.
25 Park Street
New Haven, Connecticut 06519

Ruth Lidz  

IN CONSIDERING THE BOY'S OEDIPAL TRANSITION, we wish to take you back to a time long before Oedipus escaped the patricidal jealousy of his homosexual father Laius, and survived to kill his father, albeit unknowingly, and marry his mother. For this we need simply to visit our Stone-Age contemporaries in the highlands of Papua New Guinea. Understanding their very different way of managing the boy's oedipal transition can serve to clarify and to some extent amplify our psychoanalytic concepts of this phase of a boy's development.

In a previous paper (Lidz and Lidz, 1977) we examined the initiation rituals of several Papua New Guinea cultures for the insights they provided concerning the boy's problems of gaining a firm masculine identity because of his initial symbiosis with his mother, his initial identification with her, and male envy of woman's maternal nurturant attributes and her closeness to nature. Stoller and Herdt (1982) utilized data about related ritual initiations into the male cults to differentiate the development of transsexuals from that of the New Guinea tribesmen who became fierce warriors after passing through a very prolonged period of homosexuality; both the transsexuals and the warriors start their lives in a lengthy intimate relationship with their mothers, with little intervention from their fathers.

We wish to express our deep appreciation to Gilbert Herdt, not only for his publications, but also for the time he has given to clarify various aspects of Sambia life and beliefs for us.

1While in general agreement, we find that the material as they and we present it demonstrates that the fathers (if not also the mothers) must raise the barrier or, at least, strongly reinforce it.507

[[RunningHead]]

The Course of Life: Adulthood and the Aging Process, Vol. 3

Edited by Stanley Greenspan and George Pollock. Adelphi, Md.: NIMH—Mental Health Study Center, 1980, xiv-608 pp.

Nathan Segel M.D.

Nathan Segel

Nathan P. Segel, M.D.
Farmington Hills, Michigan

Nathan Segel  

The present volume represents the last segment of a trilogy put together by these editors as an essentially psychoanalytic view of the development of human personality over the entire course of life. The first volume covered infancy and early childhood; the second included latency, adolescence, and youth. In all cases, a glittering galaxy of internationally known contributors was assembled, led in the first two books by the original contributions of Anna Freud and Erik Erikson. Most of the writers are psychoanalysts, of varying theoretical orientations, while a few distinguished colleagues represent related disciplines.

With a few exceptions that may well represent the vagaries of personal taste or bias, as the case may be, I found the general caliber of the contributions in Volume 3 of unusually high quality and scholarship, rarely found in such a collection. But what was lacking for me was an editorial screening that would have eliminated some papers, however charming, that did not particularly fit the general subject of human development in a given segment of the life span.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Dream Theory and Recent Neurobiological Findings about Rem Sleep

Marvin Wasserman M.D.

Marvin Wasserman

Marvin D. Wasserman, M.D.
320 Central Park West
New York, New York 10025

Marvin Wasserman  

SUMMARY

I have reviewed (1) Hobson and McCarley's activation-synthesis hypothesis of dreaming which attempts to show that the instigation

844

and certain formal aspects of dreaming are physiologically determined by a brainstem neuronal mechanism, (2) their reasons for suggesting major revisions in psychoanalytic dream theory, and (3) neurophysiological data that are inconsistent with their hypothesis. I then discussed the concept of mind-body isomorphism pointing out that they use this concept inconsistently, that despite their denials they regularly view physiology as primary and psychological processes as secondary, and that they frequently make the error of mixing the languages of physiology and psychology in their explanatory statements. Finally, in order to evaluate Hobson and McCarley's claim that their findings require revision of psychoanalytic dream theory, I examined their discussions of chase dreams, flying dreams, sexual dreams, the formal characteristics of dreams, the forgetting of dreams, and the instigation of dreams. I concluded that although their fascinating physiological findings may be central to understanding the neurobiology of REM sleep, they do not alter the meaning and interpretation of dreams gleaned through psychoanalytic study.

Paulina Kernberg  

SUMMARY

Matt's analysis yields a number of theoretical and clinical implications. It demonstrates that narcissistic character pathology can exist in childhood and shows how family dynamics may contribute. The clearly defensive function of Matt's pathology and his later progress through normal infantile narcissism toward further development suggest that the pathogenesis of narcissistic pathology resides neither in regression to an infantile position nor in untamed infantile narcissism. Rather, as the case demonstrates, narcissistic pathology actually reflects a pathological formation of the self used for defensive purposes. Matt's analysis also shows how such pathology in children may be effectively resolved through interpretation. Finally, the analysis demonstrates the peculiarities of the countertransference in such cases and how they may be pivotal in the course of treatment.

[[RunningHead]]

Plea for a Measure of Abnormality

By Joyce McDougall. New York: International Universities Press, 1980, 493 pp., $32.50.

Edward Joseph M.D.

Edward Joseph

Edward D. Joseph, M.D.
New York, New York

Edward Joseph  

As one of the internationally known French psychoanalysts, Joyce McDougall demonstrates in this book her grasp of clinical psychoanalysis and, more particularly, her ability to describe the clinical situation out of which such theory arises. She regards a psychoanalytic encounter as a drama that involves two people—the patient and the analyst—and a host of other dramatis personae recreated by the patient in the analytic space in which both parties operate. The re-creation of this drama allows for the interplay between patient and analyst—between transference and countertransference—bringing a vividness to the encounter and to the efforts at understanding the inner conflicts, defensive maneuvers, fantasies, and psychic pain which are integral parts of living situations. The patients she describes cover a wide range of disorders, ranging from perverse sexuality, male and female homosexuality, psychosomatic disorders, narcissistic states and finally, the least analyzable of all, normality. All her patients deal with castration anxieties as almost the least of their suffering, with the greater degree of problems arising from early primitive levels of development in which all the vicissitudes of the differentiation and fusion of the self and the other are manifest.

[[RunningHead]]

On the Analyst's Neutrality

Warren Poland M.D.

Warren Poland

Warren S. Poland, M.D.
5225 Connection Avenue, N.W.
Washington, D.C. 20015

Warren Poland  

SUMMARY

It is by the application of the principle of neutrality, born of his respect for the essential otherness of the patient, that the analyst focuses the dyadic analytic work in the service of the patient's growing self-analytic capacity. Thus, the general principle of neutrality is distinguished from the technical tactic of abstinence, the latter being a specific function utilized to facilitate and foster analytic regression.

Neutrality can be defined as it applies to the major subfunctions of the analyst's work ego. Perception of the patient's intrapsychic processes (both empathically and cognitively) requires

297

a neutrality of appearance on the analyst's part in order to minimize the distortion of the unfolding transference neurosis. Integration and understanding of the patient's communications require mastery and neutralization of the analyst's own internal processes in order to minimize countertransferential distortions. Appropriate interpretive intervention requires neutrality of action, i.e., mastery of impulses related to power, neutralizing them into the service of the analytic work; tact is defined as a specific psychoanalytic function in this regard.

Collaborative ignorance is examined as a specific instance of false neutrality. In this an analytic guise serves to mask a countertransferential conflict. Neutrality serves as an overriding technical principle, not an imperative for perfectionism. Factors intrinsic to the analytic process also influence the application of this principle.

Character: A Concept in Search of an Identity

Francis Baudry M.D.

Francis Baudry

Francis Baudry, M.D.
9 East 96th Street
New York, New York 10028

Francis Baudry  

SUMMARY

I have attempted to clarify the clinical usage of the concept of character. I have examined such terms as character trait, character, and character disorder. I have described various schemes of classification, and have proposed key definitions for the above terms. I have described the unique aspect of character, that is, its capacity to establish a relation between a superficial attribute and a deep structure. Finally, I have suggested that character represents the core of the individual from a particular point of view—a precipitate of a person's relations to the outside world.

[[RunningHead]]

Working with Shame in Psychoanalytic Treatment

Andrew Morrison M.D.

Andrew Morrison

Andrew P. Morrison, M.D.
32 Hawthorn Street
Cambridge, Massachusetts 02138

Andrew Morrison  

SUMMARY

Shame is a central human affect, reflecting feelings of defect, inferiority, and failure of the self. It is, therefore, a proper focus for psychoanalytic treatment. Beginning with Freud's seminal attention to narcissism and the ego ideal, the possibility for studying shame and its relation to the ego ideal (i.e. the loving function of the superego) was inherent in psychoanalytic theory, but Freud's pursuit of intrapsychic conflict and the punitive superego postponed further elaboration of shame. Interest in the relation of the ego ideal to the superego (Hartmann, 1950); (Reich, 1954), and in the ideal self (Sandler et al., 1963); (Schafer, 1960), (1967) opened the way to further study of shame. Kohut's contributions, with their focus on narcissism and self-pathology, have given a language and perspective on self-deficits allowing elaboration of shame's place in psychoanalytic treatment.

In this paper, I have focused on the treatment of shame in two patients. I suggest that shame lies at the very center of the narcissistic patient's pathology, with primary internal shaming (directed at the self's failures and inadequacies) permeating all aspects of the treatment. For the neurotic patient, shame is more circumscribed, reflecting partial failures of the self; it tends to be reactive, relating to passive withdrawal from internal conflict and castration fears, and is intermixed with oedipal manifestations. I have described clinical sequences that demonstrate my approach to working with shame in each of these patients. In both cases, the task is to recognize, acknowledge, accept, and investigate the patient's shame. Only after such empathic investigation can underlying conflictual and genetic derivatives be productively pursued. This sequence is often intuitively followed in analysis, but in this paper I have attempted to articulate more systematically shame's role in psychoanalytic treatment.

Some Theoretical and Technical Considerations Concerning the Emergence of a Symptom of the Transference Neurosis: An Empirical Study

Erik Gann M.D.

Erik Gann

Erik Gann, M.D.
8 East 96th Street
New York, New York 10128

Erik Gann  

SUMMARY

A detailed study was done of the five occurrences of a striking conversion symptom that appeared in the first 400 hours of an analysis. This study focused on the dynamics of the resistances and of the transference to explain why the symptom appeared when it did. It was possible to identify a consistent, repeated pattern of shifts in the resistive dynamics in response to interpretations of resistances that led to the emergence of the symptom. This type of process was viewed as demonstrating the concrete expression of the transference neurosis, distinguishable from the characteristic transferences that are seen moment-to-moment throughout a treatment from beginning to end. The implications of this distinction for the theory of the concept of the transference neurosis have been presented, emphasizing their importance in the clinical setting and for clinical theory.

[[RunningHead]]

Rational Versus Anagogic Interpretation: Xenophon's Dream and Others

Martin Stein M.D.

Martin Stein

Martin H. Stein, M.D.
8 East 96th Street
New York, New York 10028

Martin Stein  

SUMMARY

Freud's insistence that "a dream is a (disguised) fulfilment of a (suppressed or repressed) wish" has come under attack repeatedly. This principle, while never widely accepted, was nevertheless expressed, explicitly or otherwise, in the works of Homer, Herodotus, Plato, and other writers of the classical world. A detailed account of a dream of Xenophon's is employed to illustrate the capacity of a psychologically naïve Athenian writer to reject anagogic interpretation in favor of a more soundly based understanding of his own dreams. This is followed with a discussion of "dreams from above," which are most likely to raise this particular issue, one of considerable clinical and theoretical importance.

[[RunningHead]]

The Selected Papers of Margaret S. Mahler (2 Vols.)

New York: Aronson. 1979, VOL. 1, xxiii + 306 pp., VOL. 2, vi + 263 pp., $40.000.

Hans Loewald M.D.

Hans Loewald

Hans Loewald, M.D.
New Haven, Connecticut

Hans Loewald  

Prior to the publication of her Selected Papers, Margaret Mahler published two books, On Human Symbiosis and the Vicissitudes of Individuation, written in collaboration with Manuel Furer (1968),1 and The Psychological Birth of the Human Infant, written in collaboration with Fred Pine and Anni Bergman (1975)2. These books are based on and are integrations of her numerous papers written over a period of some 40 years. The developmental progression of her ideas and the detailed documentation of her therapeutic and research work can now be followed clearly, thanks to the publication of her Selected Papers.

The following pages are not intended as an overview or detailed appreciation of this series of articles. An excellent overview is provided by Marjorie Harley's and Annemarie Weil's introduction to volume 1. Extensive discussions and elaborations of Mahler's work have appeared and continue to appear in many psychoanalytic publications. Furthermore, not being a child analyst and not doing research in child development, I do not feel competent to engage in such discussions. One does not have to be an expert in her field, however, to appreciate and admire the careful, flexible design of her clinical research, the depth and scope of her psychoanalytic understanding of child behavior in interaction with the human environment, and to be fully aware of the importance of her conceptualizations and of their pertinence to the whole range of psychoanalytic work.

In the main, I shall reflect on some general issues and implications of her writings which seem to be at the center of much current analytic thinking and controversy.

1New York: International Universities Press.2New York: Basic Books.165

525 East 68th Street
New York, New York 10021

Theodore Shapiro  

PSYCHOANALYSIS RESTS ON A LONG TRADITION of constant interplay between theory and practice. Freud's writings reveal his awareness of the fact that theoretical models serve as guidelines for practice and that theory must be modified by the data of practice. Since Freud's writings were so varied and his models changed with new experience, we have had to accept and understand that psychoanalysis, as a composite, includes a number of theoretical lines or points of view, some of which are considered essential for the most complete definition of the science and others of which are considered to be dispensable for practice (Gill and Rapaport, 1959). Waelder (1962) has added a hierarchic categorization of the varying levels of theory, including clinical theory. Neutrality, as a concept, belongs to the level of clinical theory, but also has far-reaching significance for other aspects of psychology and metapsychology as they intertwine.

I believe it would be easy enough for most psychoanalysts to agree that what we do in psychoanalytic therapy with patients is in accord with our technical aims. However, even a casual perusal of psychoanalytic history reveals multiple and changing aims, justifying a brief review of how the aims of technique within psychoanalysis have changed with new challenges during these past three quarters of a century. I shall catalogue some of them in order to lend emphasis to the notion that practice and theory interact in changing contexts of need and perceived aims.

Professor of Psychiatry, Professor of Psychiatry in Pediatrics, Director, Child and Adolescent Psychiatry, Cornell University Medical College; Assistant Lecturer, New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

The End Phase of Analysis: Indicators, Functions, and Tasks of Termination

Morton Shane M.D. and Estelle Shane Ph.D.

Estelle Shane

Morton Shane, M.D.
Estelle Shane, Ph.D.
1800 Fairburn Avenue
Las Angeles, California 90025

Estelle Shane  

SUMMARY

This paper considers the end phase of analysis. Beginning with a brief review of the literature on termination, specifically the

769

indicators for initiating the termination process, we identify the structural attainments necessary for the patient to successfully complete the analysis and to maintain smooth post-analytic functioning. We stress in this regard the significance of self-analytic functions and the relative immutability of the transference neurosis. These points are illustrated with clinical examples. Our paper concludes with a discussion of the tasks and contributions of the analyst during the termination process. We make special reference to countertransference vulnerability resulting from the analyst's own termination experiences.

It has been said that Freud's case histories read like novels. Sours has written a novel that should be read as a case history. This excessively discursive volume presents the author's continuing efforts to convey his particular perspective of the intricacies of communication in the therapeutic dyad. Over two thirds consist of Langs's edited transcripts of his audio-taped teaching seminars in psychotherapy for residency-level trainees in an outpatient clinical setting. Each of the seminars is designated as a chapter and titled according to salient themes embedded in Langs's free-form commentary. To these are appended an excellent bibliography and three quite different addenda: the first a preprint of a concurrent article, "Some communicative properties of the bi-personal field"; the second a four-page outline of the components of the listening process; the last a glossary of special terms, coined or derived, that must be mastered in order to follow the author in his formulations.

[[RunningHead]]

Attachment and Loss, Vol. 3. Loss, Sadness and Depression

By John Bowlby. New York. Basic Books, 1980, 472 pp., $22.50.

Sol Altschul M.D.

Sol Altschul

Sol Altschul, M.D.
Chicago, Illinois

Sol Altschul  

This book, the final volume of three on the subjects of attachment, separation, and loss, addresses itself to the subject of loss and its consequences.

It is Bowlby's intent to address the controversies that have arisen in psychiatric and psychoanalytic thinking about the questions of depression and childhood mourning, to present his view of mourning and the defensive processes involved derived from studies in cognitive psychology and information theory, and to demonstrate "the influences on responses to loss of the experiences which a bereaved person has had with attachment figures during the whole course of his life and especially during his infancy, childhood and adolescence. These experiences are held to account for a large proportion of the variance observed in the course taken by mourning in adults" (pp. 76-77).

In developing his ideas and the central theme that even young children can mourn, Bowlby presents an erudite comprehensive review of the literature on the mourning processes, normal and pathological, in adults and children. In the course of his review, Bowlby demonstrates what he considers to be the similarities in reactions to loss in adults and children. He delineates the typical sequence of numbness, yearning, disorganization, despair, and reorganization. He then illustrates that the pathological variants take the form of exaggerations or elaborations of these processes that are present in and characterize normal mourning. These variants in turn are manifested in expressions of chronic mourning or prolonged absence of grief. In effect, it is not the presence of such phenomena that indicates pathology but the "scope, intensity and persistence" of these phenomena beyond reasonable duration.

One might very briefly summarize the main points of Bowlby's work as follows: (1) The kind of attachment behavior that has been established determines the reactions to loss. (2) Defensive exclusion is the main mechanism of pathological formation excluding information from awareness and leading to pathological formation. (3) Adults and children mourn in essentially the same way with the same sequence of reactions. (4) Pathological consequences are the result of persistent exaggerations of normal phenomena and are more likely to occur in childhood bereavements. (5) Familial aftercare is the most important factor in the outcome of the child's bereavement. (6) The term mourning should not be restricted to cases of favorable outcome.

Jacob Arlow  

FUNDAMENTALLY, OURS IS A SCIENTIFIC JOURNAL. It represents the contribution we as a profession make to the advancement of human knowledge; in our case it also holds out the promise of healing troubled spirits. Thus, our journal is not only a medium for communicating ideas within our discipline, but it is also the interface of psychoanalysis with the world of science and with the public at large. Over this grave responsibility presides the Editor of the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association. We should congratulate each other and thank the Search Committee who some dozen years ago recognized real quality in a comparatively unknown, truly young man in psychoanalysis, and nominated him for the post of Editor. We must also thank the Executive Council for confirming the appointment with swiftness and effectiveness.

In spite of the many ways the Editor is involved with coeditors, authors, office staff, publisher, and so forth, being Editor is fundamentally a lonely job. The responsibility is awesome, for he is the final arbiter of what ideas see the light of day and become part of the general domain of psychoanalytic thought. Exercising such authority to a very considerable degree, the Editor sets his imprint, his views, and his understanding on the nature and direction of psychoanalysis.

Presented at a dinner honoring Dr. Blum, Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 17, 1983.

[[RunningHead]]

Object and Self: A Developmental Approach

Edited by Saul Tuttman, Carol Kaye, and Muriel Zimmerman. New York: International Universities Press, 1980, 548 pp., $40.00.

Nathan Schlessinger M.D.

Nathan Schlessinger

Nathan Schlessinger, M.D.
Chicago, Illinois

Nathan Schlessinger  

These essays, in honor of Edith Jacobson, constitute more a mosaic than an integrated view of critique or the theoretical, clinical, and research issues involved. The effort to be comprehensive and respectful of differences is clearly evident and useful. In such a complex field, the task of a critical review remains inevitably that of each reader. The volume would have benefited from a continuing editorial commentary in the elucidation of the issues and explanatory proposals.

In the historical survey, Tuttman notes the roots of an object-relations theory in Freud: types of object choice, the nature of internalization in depression, mourning and normal development, and the concept of splitting of the ego in fetishism and in the psychoneuroses. He touches on Sandor Ferenczi's therapeutic dyad, Karl Abraham's evolution of object love paralleling the libidinal phases, Federn's views of depersonalization, ego states, and self-feelings. He reviews the contributions of Melanie Klein and the British object-relations school, posing many of the questions about a preverbal archaic phase of development for analysis versus the analysis of a structured personality: the objects real and fantasied in the child's traumatic experiences, the significance of instinct versus relation to objects, the provision of a holding environment versus the interpretation of archaic fantasies and affects. He summarizes the integrative concepts of Erik Erikson, D. W. Winnicott and Margaret Mahler.

The contributions of Edith Jacobson are set forth against this historical backdrop. Tuttman suggests that she provided a unifying developmental schema, an object-relations perspective, to study the origins and qualities of self and object, drawing from clinical experience and existing metapsychology. Jacob Arlow cites her courage and clinical acumen, demonstrated under severe stress in her observation of regression in captivity in Nazi Germany among her fellow captives and her jailers. He notes that her study of regression in self and superego revealed that they were not simply instinctual and emphasized the importance of identification as an explanatory concept.
